items.title: Add hbctool._bitcodec C++ extension and route integer encode/decode through it
items.body:
### Motivation

- Reduce Python-level overhead for small integer/byte conversions by moving little-endian integer encode/decode paths to native code for speed and lower CPU cost.

### Description

- Added a new C++ extension `hbctool/_bitcodec.cpp` which implements `le_to_uint` and `uint_to_le` helpers for little-endian integer decoding/encoding up to 8 bytes.
- Wired `hbctool/util.py` to discover/load the new `_bitcodec` extension when `HBCTOOL_FASTUTIL=1` and to route `readuint` and `writeuint` through `_bitcodec` for sizes up to 8 bytes (falling back to the Python code when the extension is not available).
- Updated `setup.py` to build and ship both `hbctool._fastutil` and the new `hbctool._bitcodec` extensions.

### Testing

- Ran the full test suite with `python -m pytest -q`, which passed (`19 passed`).
- Built extensions with `python setup.py build_ext --inplace`, which succeeded and produced both `_fastutil` and `_bitcodec` shared objects (note: an existing compiler warning from `_fastutil.cpp` remains). 
- Exercised the fast runtime path by running `HBCTOOL_FASTUTIL=1 python -m pytest -q tests/test_util.py`, which revealed two failing tests due to a pre-existing exception-type mismatch in `_fastutil.to_uint16` error handling (the failure is unrelated to `_bitcodec` logic).

S-tier (10-50x speedup potential):

items.title: Add Doodle cpp binding
items.body:
This pull request adds native support for rendering Doodle origami diagram files to PostScript directly from Python, and introduces a robust build and packaging system for cross-platform distribution. The main changes include integration of the Doodle C++ codebase as a submodule, new Python bindings via pybind11, high-level Python APIs for rendering, and automated build workflows for wheels and source distributions.

**Native Doodle integration and rendering support:**

* Added the Doodle C++ project as a submodule (`doodle`) and configured it in `.gitmodules`. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-3bfc46640be4fe9e435f96aef61db5a6bacff92ba0198546f8ca321542980946R1) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-fe7afb5c9c916e521401d3fcfb4277d5071798c3baf83baf11d6071742823584R1-R3)
* Implemented a pybind11 extension (`pydoodle/_bindings.cpp`) to expose the Doodle parse/render pipeline to Python, allowing `.doo` files to be rendered to PostScript from Python.
* Provided a high-level Python API (`pydoodle/render.py`) with `render` and `render_file` functions for converting `Diagram` objects or `.doo` files to PostScript.
* Updated the package interface (`pydoodle/__init__.py`) to expose the new rendering functions. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-e655c067101d793da8f44b83c5cf88998b967dcf7d03e9dd252b284c0b3a45c7R3) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-e655c067101d793da8f44b83c5cf88998b967dcf7d03e9dd252b284c0b3a45c7R86-R87)

**Build and packaging system:**

* Added `setup.py` to build the C++ extension using pybind11 and setuptools, including handling for generated parser/lexer files and cross-platform compiler settings.
* Introduced `pyproject.toml` for modern PEP 517/518 build configuration, specifying dependencies and cibuildwheel settings for multi-version Python wheels.
* Created a GitHub Actions workflow (`.github/workflows/build.yml`) to automate building wheels and source distributions for all major platforms and publishing to PyPI on tagged releases.

**Documentation improvements:**

* Extended `README.md` with detailed build, installation, and parser regeneration instructions, including platform-specific notes for developers.

items.body:
https://github.com/walt-id/waltid-identity-enterprise/pull/382

- Multiple credentials for Annex C DC API
- Reader Auth (for multiple credentials) for Annex C DC API
- Various smaller fixes and changes for Verifier2

- This branch is based on https://github.com/walt-id/waltid-identity/pull/1590 and https://github.com/walt-id/waltid-identity/pull/1589 (primarily adding support for custom credential issuance (non-typesafe credentials) in the mdocs2 library, and updating the claim handling to no longer require explicit schemas upfront for received credentials)
  - Mdocs schema aware issuance
  - Massively rehaul CBOR handling, simplify serialization and deserialization (including removing multiple custom serializers), robust handling of non-primitives, better CBOR tagging support
  - Rethrow error to avoid sending random 404 responses with no data
  - Remove incorrect OpenAPI parameter for session info
  - Fix buildFarJar for hoplite

- Adds support for using the normal GeneralFlowConfig options for Annex C (e.g. for webhook notifications)
- Removed random wrappers in Annex C flow config, so that it can be used almost the same as the other flow configs (e.g. regarding policies)

---
Changes API of Annex C in Verifier2 slightly to support multiple credentials, new API is:
```json
{
  "flow_type": "dc_api-annex-c",
  "requestedElements": {
    "org.iso.18013.5.1.mDL": {
      "org.iso.18013.5.1": [
        "family_name",
        "..."
      ]
    }
  },
  "origin": "https://digital-credentials.walt.id"
}
```
Note how there is no `"docType"` attribute, and instead the credentials are named in `requestedElements` (which also feels more natural, as this is also the format that they are being represented with in other parts).

items.title: Use PETSc CXX for C++ extension builds
items.body:
## Summary
- read `CXX` from PETSc `petscvariables` in `setup.py`
- export `CXX` alongside the existing `CC` override

## Why this is important
Pre-fix, `setup.py` only forced PETSc's `CC`. That meant C builds followed PETSc, but C++ builds could still fall back to the ambient environment compiler.

In practice, this can send `underworld3.ckdtree` through a different or broken C++ toolchain than the PETSc/MPI build, especially in Pixi/OpenMPI environments.

Using both PETSc `CC` and `CXX` keeps the Underworld extension build aligned with the PETSc toolchain.

## Validation
- `python3 -m py_compile setup.py`
- `OMPI_CC=/usr/bin/clang OMPI_CXX=/usr/bin/clang++ pixi run -e amr-dev pip install . --no-build-isolation`

<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

## Overview

Mirror the build system changes from the MBFVAR repository (commit `c29effc`) into SBFVAR. The goal is to switch from `hatchling` to `setuptools` with pybind11/numpy build requirements so that C++ extensions are compiled from source at install time, remove all pre-compiled `.so` files and build artifacts from git tracking, fix a module naming bug in `solve.cpp`, and add comprehensive documentation.

## Changes Required

### 1. Update `pyproject.toml`

Replace the current hatchling-based build system with setuptools + pybind11 + numpy. The new file should be:

```toml
[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools", "pybind11", "numpy"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[tool.setuptools.packages.find]
include = ["SBFVAR*"]

[project]
name = "SBFVAR"
version = "0.1.2"
dependencies = [
"numpy",
"scipy",
"pandas",
"datetime",
"matplotlib",
"tqdm",
"plotly",
"fanchart",
"openpyxl",
"xlsxwriter",
"bayesian-optimization",
"arm-mango",
"scikit-learn"
]
requires-python = ">= 3.8"
authors = [
  {name = "Laurent Florin", email = "laurent.florin@efv.admin.ch"}]
description = "Single Base Frequency Bayesian VAR Model"
readme = "README.md"
classifiers = [
  "Development Status :: 3 - Alpha",
  "Programming Language :: Python",
]
[project.urls]
Repository = "https://github.com/laurentflorin/SBFVAR.git"
```

### 2. Add root-level `setup.py`

Create a new `setup.py` at the repository root that defines all four C++ pybind11 extensions. The Eigen headers are vendored at `SBFVAR/third_party/eigen`. The extensions are:

- `SBFVAR.cholcov.cholcov_module` — sources: `SBFVAR/cholcov/cholcov.cpp`, `SBFVAR/cholcov/cholcov_bindings.cpp`
- `SBFVAR.inverse.matrix_inversion` — sources: `SBFVAR/inverse/matrix_inversion.cpp`
- `SBFVAR.pseudo_inverse.pseudo_inverse` — sources: `SBFVAR/pseudo_inverse/pseudo_inverse.cpp`, `SBFVAR/pseudo_inverse/pseudo_inverse_bindings.cpp`
- `SBFVAR.solve.solve` — sources: `SBFVAR/solve/solve.cpp`

All extensions should include `pybind11.get_include()` and the vendored Eigen include directory. Use `pathlib.Path` to resolve the Eigen path relative to the setup.py location. Example:

```python
from setuptools import setup, Extension
from pathlib import Path
import pybind11

eigen_include = Path(__file__).resolve().parent / "SBFVAR" / "third_party" / "eigen"

ext_modules = [
    Extension(
        "SBFVAR.cholcov.cholcov_module",
        sources=[
            "SBFVAR/cholcov/cholcov.cpp",
            "SBFVAR/cholcov/cholcov_bindings.cpp",
        ],
        include_dirs=[pybind11.get_include(), str(eigen_include)],
        language="c++",
    ),
    Extension(
        "SBFVAR.inverse.matrix_inversion",
        sources=["SBFVAR/inverse/matrix_inversion.cpp"],
        include_dirs=[pybind11.get_include(), str(eigen_include)],
        language="c++",
    ),
    Extension(
        "SBFVAR.pseudo_inverse.pseudo_inverse",
        sources=[
            "SBFVAR/pseudo_inverse/pseudo_inverse.cpp",
            "SBFVAR/pseudo_inverse/pseudo_inverse_bindings.cpp",
        ],
        include_dirs=[pybind11.get_include(), str(eigen_include)],
        language="c++",
    ),
    Extension(
        "SBFVAR.solve.solve",
        sources=["SBFVAR/solve/solve.cpp"],
        include_dirs=[pybind11.get_include(), str(eigen_include)],
        language="c++",
    ),
]

setup(ext_modules=ext_modules)
```

### 3. Fix `PYBIND11_MODULE` name in `SBFVAR/solve/solve.cpp`

The current file uses `PYBIND11_MODULE(linalg_solve, m)` but the extension is named `SBFVAR.solve.solve`, so the module name in the macro **must** be `solve`. Change the `PYBIND11_MODULE` line from:

```cpp
PYBIND11_MODULE(linalg_solve, m) {
```

to:

```cpp
PYBIND11_MODULE(solve, m) {
```

The function name `linalg_solve` inside remains unchanged — only the module name changes.

**IMPORTANT**: After this rename, check `SBFVAR/_estimation.py` (and any other Python files) for imports of `linalg_solve` as a module. Any import like `from .solve.linalg_solve import linalg_solve` or `from SBFVAR.solve import linalg_solve` must be updated to `from .solve.solve import linalg_solve` or `from SBFVAR.solve.solve import linalg_solve`. Search for all occurrences of `linalg_solve` in `.py` files and update the module import path accordingly while keeping the function name `linalg_solve` as-is.

### 4. Update `.gitignore`

Replace/update the `.gitignore` to exclude compiled extensions and build artifacts:

```gitignore
# Python
*.pyc
__pycache__/
*.egg-info/
*.egg
dist/
build/

# Compiled extensions
*.so
*.pyd
*.dll

# IDE
.vscode/
.idea/

# OS
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# R artifacts
.RData
.Rhistory
```

### 5. Remove pre-compiled `.so` files and build artifacts from git

Delete ALL of the following files/directories from the repository (they are currently tracked in git but should not be, since extensions will be compiled from source at install time):

**Pre-compiled .so files to delete:**
- `SBFVAR/cholcov/cholcov_module.cpython-310-x86_64-linux-gnu.so`
- `SBFVAR/cholcov/ch...

items.title: Add DECIMAL Type Support to C# Language Extension
items.body:
## Summary

This PR implements full support for SQL Server `DECIMAL` type in the C# language extension, enabling seamless conversion between SQL Server's 19-byte `SQL_NUMERIC_STRUCT` format and .NET's `SqlDecimal` type. The implementation introduces a new `SqlNumericHelper` utility class and simplifies existing code by leveraging `SqlDecimal`'s built-in capabilities.

## Why These Changes?

**Problem**: The C# language extension lacked proper support for SQL Server's `DECIMAL` types, which are critical for financial, scientific, and precision-sensitive applications. Without this support:
- Stored procedures and user-defined functions using `DECIMAL` parameters couldn't be called from C# extensions
- Result sets containing `DECIMAL` columns couldn't be properly processed
- OUTPUT parameters of type `DECIMAL` returned incorrect or corrupted values

**Solution**: Implement bidirectional conversion between SQL Server's native `SQL_NUMERIC_STRUCT` (ODBC 19-byte format) and .NET's `SqlDecimal` type, with proper handling of:
- Precision (1-38 digits) and scale (0-precision)
- NULL values using `SqlDecimal.Null`
- OUTPUT parameter sentinel detection (uninitialized structs with precision=0 used as a sentinel/marker value to indicate OUTPUT parameters)
- Signed values (positive/negative decimals)

## What Changed?

#### 1. **SqlNumericHelper.cs**
Created a comprehensive utility class for DECIMAL conversions with five core methods:

- **`ToSqlDecimal(SqlNumericStruct)`**: Converts SQL 19-byte struct → `SqlDecimal`
  - Handles sign bit, precision, scale validation
  - Reconstructs 128-bit value from four 32-bit integers
  
- **`FromSqlDecimal(SqlDecimal, precision, scale)`**: Converts `SqlDecimal` to SQL struct

items.title: Add optional C++ Huffman extension and integrate parallel decompression
items.body:
### Motivation
- Improve performance of Huffman symbol encode/decode and multi-block layer decompression by providing a native C++ implementation and using it when available.
- Reduce Python-level decoding work and enable parallel decompression of layers to accelerate batch cache reconstruction.

### Description
- Add a new C++ extension `huffman/huffman_ext.cpp` exposing `huffman_encode_symbols_cpp`, `huffman_decode_symbols_cpp`, and `decompress_layer_parallel_cpp` via pybind11 for CPU-optimized operations.
- Make the extension optional in `huffman/huffman_compressor.py` by attempting `from ._C import ...` and falling back to the pure-Python implementations when the extension is not present (`_HAS_HUFFMAN_EXT` flag).
- Use the C++ extension in `batch_decompress` to decode layers in parallel using `decompress_layer_parallel_cpp` and reconstruct BF16 bit patterns directly into NumPy arrays, with a Python fallback that uses `ThreadPoolExecutor` and `_decompress_chunk`.
- Accelerate per-symbol operations by routing `_huffman_encode_symbols` and `_huffman_decode_symbols` to the corresponding C++ functions when available, while preserving the original Python implementations as fallbacks.
- Update `setup.py` to build the C++ extension by adding a `CppExtension` target for `huffman._C` compiling `huffman/huffman_ext.cpp` with optimization flags.

### Testing
- No automated test suite was executed as part of this change.

items.title: Add support for rosidl::Buffer in rosidl C path for rclpy
items.body:
## Description

This pull request makes the rosidl C layer Buffer-aware and adds Python bindings for `rosidl::Buffer`, enabling rclpy message types to work with vendor-backed buffer memory. 

This pull request consists of the following key changes:

- **`rosidl_runtime_c`**: Adds `is_rosidl_buffer` field (bool) in each primitive sequence struct (e.g., `rosidl_runtime_c__uint8__Sequence`). When true, `data` points to an `rosidl::Buffer<T>*`.
- **`rosidl_generator_c`**: For messages with `uint8[]` fields, the generated fini logic (`msg__functions.c.em`) checks `msg->field.is_rosidl_buffer` and clears the sequence fields without calling normal sequence fini, avoiding double-free of a borrowed `rosidl::Buffer*` used by the Python C extension.
- **`rosidl_typesupport_introspection_c`**: The goal for the changes in C introspection is to keep it working with CPU-based path. The introspection accessors throw exceptions for non-CPU backends.
- **`rosidl_buffer_py`** (new): Python bindings (pybind11) for `rosidl::Buffer`.

### Is this user-facing behavior change?

This pull request does not change existing rclpy behavior. The `is_rosidl_buffer` flag defaults to false, so all existing C sequence code paths are unchanged. The `rosidl_buffer_py.Buffer` class is new and only used when vendor-backed (non-CPU) buffers are received by rclpy subscribers. CPU-based data continues to be delivered as `array.array`.

### Did you use Generative AI?

<!--
If this pull request was generated using Generative AI, please specify the tool and model used (e.g., GitHub Copilot, GPT-4.1).
If only specific parts were generated by Generative AI, please list those parts (e.g., function A, class B, etc.).
-->

Yes. Claude (claude-4.6-opus) via Cursor was used to assist with creating an initial prototype version of the changes contained in this PR.

### Additional Information

<!--
If applicable, provide any additional context or details about the changes.
-->

This PR is part of the broader ROS 2 native buffer feature introduced in this [post](https://discourse.openrobotics.org/t/working-prototype-of-native-buffers-accelerated-memory-transport/52399).

It depends on PRs #941, #942 and https://github.com/ros2/rosidl_typesupport_fastrtps/pull/144 (core buffer types, C++ rosidl changes, and C++ serialization).

## Summary

- **MLX backend** (`turbomoq/mlx_backend.py`) — Metal GPU acceleration for rotation, quantize, and Lloyd-Max codebook. Overtakes numpy at dim ≥ 2048 on M4. Cosine agreement = 1.000000 with numpy backend.
- **Attention-weighted tier demotion** (`turbomoq/attention_demotion.py`) — 5-tier system that assigns compression based on actual attention scores, not token age. EMA tracking, pin protection for high-value tokens. 4.2x compression at avg 3.8 bits.
- **QJL residual correction** (`turbomoq/qjl.py`) — 1-bit sign-encoded JL projection for sub-2-bit recovery. Per-vector scale correction + multi-round mode. +3.1% cosine at 1-bit base quantization.

items.title: [ENG-488] Add C/C++ collector + policy
items.body:
## What's included

- `collectors/cpp/lunar-collector.yml` — Plugin manifest with 5 sub-collectors
- `collectors/cpp/README.md` — Documentation
- `collectors/cpp/assets/cpp.svg` — Icon (fill=black, from simple-icons)

## Design summary

Writes to `.lang.cpp` following the established language collector pattern (matches Go, Java, .NET structure).

### Sub-collectors

| Name | Hook | What it does |
|------|------|-------------|
| `project` | code | Detects build systems (CMake, Make, Meson, Autotools, Bazel), source file counts, C++ standard version, package manager config |
| `dependencies` | code | Extracts deps from Conan (`conanfile.txt`/`conanfile.py`), vcpkg (`vcpkg.json`), and CMake `find_package()` |
| `cppcheck` | code | Optional static analysis — skips gracefully if cppcheck not installed |
| `cicd` | ci-before-command | Tracks compiler commands (gcc, g++, clang, clang++) with version |
| `cmake-cicd` | ci-before-command | Tracks cmake commands with version |

### Component JSON paths

- `.lang.cpp` — project metadata, build systems, source file counts, C++ standard
- `.lang.cpp.dependencies` — direct deps + cmake packages
- `.lang.cpp.lint` — normalized cppcheck warnings
- `.lang.cpp.native.cppcheck` — raw cppcheck output
- `.lang.cpp.cicd` — compiler/cmake commands from CI

## Relationship to existing plugins

- Follows the same `.lang.<language>` pattern as Go, Java, .NET, Rust, Python, PHP, Node.js
- No existing C/C++ collector — this is net-new
- No policy included per ticket scope ("Policy can be added later")
- cppcheck sub-collector mirrors the golangci-lint pattern from the Go collector

## Testing plan

- **Project detection:** Test against repos with CMake, Make, and bare C/C++ source files
- **Dependencies:** Test against repos with conanfile.txt, vcpkg.json, and CMakeLists.txt with find_package()
- **cppcheck:** Test with cppcheck installed (expect lint results) and without (expect graceful skip)
- **CI collectors:** Test on cronos with gcc/clang compile steps in GitHub Actions
- **Edge cases:** No C/C++ files (should write nothing), mixed C/C++ and other languages

## Open questions

1. Should `make` commands be tracked in CI? Decided against it since `make` is too generic (used for non-C/C++ too). Open to adding if reviewers disagree.
2. The `base-main` image should suffice for project/dependencies. cppcheck can be installed via `apk add` in the script. Worth creating a dedicated `cpp-main` image instead?

items.title: feat(perf)!: replace Cython with hand-written C extension and optimize codegen
items.body:
## Summary
- Replace Cython-generated wrapper with a hand-written `METH_O`/`METH_NOARGS` C extension
- Eliminates keyword arg parsing, RefNanny, stack canary, and two-function dispatch overhead from every call
- Removes Cython as a build dependency
- Optimized uint128 codegen: `MarshalTo` decomposes into register-local `uint64_t` halves (eliminates ~24 redundant memory loads), `MarshalBinaryTo` uses `__builtin_bswap64` (4 instructions vs 16 shifts + 16 vector inserts)
- Direct base32 encode/decode between bytes and strings without intermediate uint128/struct representation
- Shared `ulid_base32.hh` header eliminates code duplication between struct and uint128 paths
- Removed `ulid_wrapper.cpp`/`.h` indirection layer — `_ulid_impl.cpp` calls `ulid::` functions directly

## Breaking change
Functions that previously accepted keyword arguments via Cython (e.g. `ulid_at_time(timestamp=1.0)`) now only accept positional arguments. This is unlikely to affect any real-world usage since these are all single-argument functions that are naturally called positionally.

## Test plan

- [x] Safety audit of the C extension (reference counting, error handling, buffer bounds)
- [x] Verified improved codegen via `otool -tV` disassembly

items.title: THRIFT-5943: optimize python C extension readStruct for nested structs
items.body:
In this area of code, there already exists an optimization where we skip calling `klass(**kwargs)` on the top-level struct and instead init an empty object then `PyObject_SetAttr` the properties onto it.  This was done because Python's keyword argument matching is O(n) string comparisons per argument, making this expensive for structs with many fields.

However, nested structs which are decoded within the C extension did not get this same optimization, causing them to go through the slow class initialization path.

In this change, for mutable nested structs, we create the instance up front with a no-arg constructor (`klass()`) and set decoded fields directly via `PyObject_SetAttr` (the same way we fast-path the top-level struct).

Immutable structs (`TFrozenBase` subclasses) continue to use the kwargs path since their generated `__setattr__` blocks attribute mutation.

Immutability is detected via (1) `PyObject_IsSubclass` against `TFrozenBase` with the class reference cached in a function-local static or (2) and overridden `__setattr__`.

Benchmarks show up to 3.6x speedup for deeply nested struct hierarchies, with no impact on flat structs. I had Claude generate me some benchmarks to showcase performance in different nested scenarios:

https://gist.github.com/markjm/e8610298d30d32869f46cf521272c85c

## Checklist

- [x] Did you create an [Apache Jira](https://issues.apache.org/jira/projects/THRIFT/issues/) ticket?  ([Request account here](https://selfserve.apache.org/jira-account.html), not required for trivial changes)

items.title: Claude/convert to python streamlit ujhj c
Add Python conversion of Qt C++ TagLister
items.body:
Converts `TagLister.h` / `TagLister.cpp` (Qt C++) to idiomatic Python as `convert/TagLister.py`. TagLister scans raw XHTML source and produces an ordered flat list of `TagInfo` records — one per tag in document order — used for structural navigation of XHTML content.

## Key translation decisions

- **`TagInfo` / `AttInfo`** — C++ nested structs become `@dataclass` types
- **Qt string types** — `QString`, `QStringView`, `QStringList` all collapse to `str` / `list[str]`; `QStringView::isNull()` → `is None`
- **`QList` ops** — `<<` / `takeLast()` / `removeLast()` / `last()` → `append()` / `pop()` / `[-1]`
- **`parseAttribute`** — C++ took `AttInfo&` by reference; Python version returns a new `AttInfo` instead
- **Private methods** — C++ `private:` block becomes `_`-prefixed methods
- **Error output** — `qDebug()` → `print(..., file=sys.stderr)`

## Usage

```python
from convert.TagLister import TagLister

lister = TagLister(xhtml_source)
for i in range(lister.size()):
    ti = lister.at(i)
    print(ti.tname, ti.ttype, ti.pos, ti.tpath)

# Attribute extraction
ainfo = TagLister.parseAttribute('<div class="foo">', "class")
# ainfo.avalue == "foo"
```

items.title: fix(languages): enable TSX, C/C++, C# in binary; uniform language coverage
items.body:
## Summary

Four of the eleven advertised languages (TSX, C, C++, C#) were silently
disabled in the published MCP binary due to missing feature flags in
`crates/code-analyze-mcp/Cargo.toml`. The core library fully implemented
all 11 languages; the binary only shipped 7. Any user passing `.tsx`,
`.c`, `.cc`, `.cpp`, or `.cs` files received an `unsupported extension`
error from all four MCP tools despite the tool descriptions advertising
support for those languages.

This PR fixes the root cause and closes all related uniformity gaps
identified in the language audit.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `crates/code-analyze-mcp/Cargo.toml` | Add `lang-tsx`, `lang-cpp`, `lang-csharp` to `code-analyze-core` features list |
| `crates/code-analyze-core/src/languages/javascript.rs` | Remove redundant per-item `#[cfg(feature = "lang-javascript")]` guards; add doc comment explaining `reference_query: None` rationale |
| `crates/code-analyze-core/src/languages/csharp.rs` | Add `struct_declaration` and `enum_declaration` to `ELEMENT_QUERY` |
| `crates/code-analyze-core/src/languages/typescript.rs` | Add named sub-captures (`@func_name`, `@class_name`, etc.) to all `ELEMENT_QUERY` node types; add unit tests |
| `crates/code-analyze-core/src/languages/java.rs` | Add unit tests (element query + `extract_inheritance`) |
| `crates/code-analyze-core/src/languages/python.rs` | Add unit tests (element query + `extract_inheritance`) |
| `crates/code-analyze-core/tests/integration_tests.rs` | Add integration tests for `.tsx`, `.c`, `.cpp`, and C# struct/enum extraction |
| `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` | Document JavaScript `reference_query: None` rationale; update language count to 11 |

## Test plan

- [x] 315 tests pass (was 303; +12 new tests)
- [x] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` clean
- [x] `cargo fmt --check` clean
- [x] `cargo deny check advisories licenses` clean
- [x] GPG signed, DCO signed-off

## Notes

C/C++ integration tests assert struct/class extraction rather than function
extraction. This is a pre-existing parser limitation: `function_definition`
in C/C++ uses a `declarator` chain rather than a `name` field, so
`child_by_field_name("name")` returns `None` in the main parser path. The
tests are scoped to what actually works and document the limitation.

items.title: Modernize CMake build: bump CMake, use Python3/Torch CMake config, replace vendored FindCUDA, update pybind11 usage
items.body:
### Motivation
- Modernize and simplify the build system to use a recent CMake, PyTorch's exported CMake targets, and a modern pybind11 integration so the project builds reliably as a Python C++ extension. 
- Remove a large legacy vendored `FindCUDA.cmake` and rely on CMake's builtin CUDA support to reduce maintenance and compatibility issues. 

### Description
- Updated `CMakeLists.txt` to require `cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.22)`, renamed the project to `tetranerf_cpp_extension`, defaulted `CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE` to `Release`, and added `find_package(Python3 COMPONENTS Interpreter REQUIRED)` with `PYTHON_EXECUTABLE` selection. 
- Switched pybind11 to `FetchContent` with `v2.11.1` and used `pybind11_add_module` to create the `tetranerf_cpp_extension` module from `src/triangulation.cpp` and `src/py_binding.cpp`, set C++17, platform-specific compile options (`/bigobj` on MSVC, `-O3` otherwise), and configured output directories. 
- Integrated PyTorch via a new `cmake/FindTorch.cmake` that queries the active Python environment, appends Torch's CMake prefix to `CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH`, runs `find_package(Torch CONFIG REQUIRED)`, discovers include/library paths via `torch.utils.cpp_extension`, and exposes `TORCH_LIBRARIES`, `TORCH_INCLUDE_DIRS`, `TORCH_LIBRARY_DIRS`, `TORCH_COMPILE_OPTIONS`, and `TORCH_COMPILE_DEFINITIONS` for target consumption. 
- Replaced the large vendored `cmake/FindCUDA.cmake` with a small wrapper that includes CMake's builtin `FindCUDA.cmake` to force use of the standard implementation expected by modern toolchains and by Torch's CMake scripts. 

### Testing
- No automated tests were run.

items.title: Reject duplicate singleton headers in C extension parser

labels.name: bot:chronographer:provided
labels.description: There is a change note present in this PR

labels.name: backport-3.13

labels.name: backport-3.14
labels.description: Trigger automatic backporting to the 3.14 release branch by Patchback robot

items.body:
## What do these changes do?

Add duplicate singleton header validation to the C extension HTTP parser (`_http_parser.pyx`) to match the existing pure Python parser behavior.

The C extension parser now rejects requests containing duplicate RFC 9110 singleton headers (`Host`, `Content-Type`, `Content-Length`, `Content-Location`, `Content-Range`, `ETag`, `Max-Forwards`, `Server`, `Transfer-Encoding`, `User-Agent`) with a `BadHttpMessage` error, just as the pure Python parser already does.

## Are there changes in behavior for the user?

Yes. HTTP requests with duplicate singleton headers that were previously accepted (and silently used the first value) are now rejected with a `400 Bad Request`. This is the **correct behavior** per RFC 9110 §5.5 and matches what the pure Python parser already enforces.

## Is it a substantial burden for the maintainers to support this?

No. The change mirrors existing logic from the pure Python parser (`http_parser.py:500-514`) into the C extension parser. It is a small, focused security fix with no new dependencies or architectural changes.

## Related issue number

Align C and python implementations

- [x] I think the code is well written
- [x] Unit tests for the changes exist
- [ ] Documentation reflects the changes
- [ ] If you provide code modification, please add yourself to `CONTRIBUTORS.txt`

## What changed

## Why it changed
- the repo already had a recovered C baseline and a narrow bootstrap executable, but it did not yet have an explicit parallel C++ executable-regeneration track
- this patch makes the executable-link surface explicit, keeps the existing C milestone green, and adds the first conservative C++ class seams without pretending the deeper startup/runtime crash band is already solved
- `clash95_cpp_regen` is intentionally built on the existing bootstrap wedge so the first C++ executable target is honest about current evidence and current limits

items.body:
Tier 16 loader conversions: MDXLoader (917-line Warcraft III MDX binary format parser) and WDTLoader (105-line WDT world table parser). Progress: 93→95/184 files.

## MDXLoader

- 20+ typed structs (`MDXNode`, `MDXBone`, `MDXGeoset`, `MDXParticleEmitter2`, `MDXCamera`, `MDXLight`, `MDXRibbonEmitter`, etc.) with `MDXAnimValue = std::variant<float, vector<float>, int32_t>`
- All 21 chunk handlers ported (VERS, MODL, SEQS, GLBS, MTLS, TEXS, GEOS, GEOA, BONE, HELP, ATCH, PIVT, EVTS, HTST, CLID, PREM, PRE2, CAMS, LITE, TXAN, RIBB)
- Both geoset parsers: `_parse_geosets_v1300()` and `_parse_geosets_v1500()` including v1500 bone lookup deduplication with trailing-zero trimming
- JS `-1` sentinel values → `std::optional<int32_t>`; dynamic objects → typed structs
- Node registration deferred to after all parsing completes to avoid dangling pointers (JS stores references; C++ objects move into vectors, invalidating addresses taken during `_read_node()`)

### Bug fix: ATCH handler

Original JS calls `this.data.readUInt32LE(-4)` which passes `-4` as count to `_readInt()`, hitting `new Array(-4)` → `RangeError`. Fixed by saving the size read at loop start:

```cpp
const uint32_t attachmentSize = this->data.readUInt32LE();
// ...
if (this->data.offset() < startPos + attachmentSize) {  // was readUInt32LE(-4)
```

## WDTLoader

- `WDTEntry` and `WDTWorldModelPlacement` structs for MAID/MODF chunks
- 5 chunk handlers (MPHD, MAIN, MAID, MWMO, MODF) using `constants::GAME::MAP_SIZE`/`MAP_SIZE_SQ`

## Housekeeping

- CMakeLists.txt: added both source files
- CONVERSION_TRACKER.md: marked both `[x]`, updated count
- AUDIT_TRACKER.md: documented `readUInt32LE(-4)` bugfix and structural deviations
- Verified: zero errors/warnings on both GCC (Linux) and MSVC (Windows CI)

items.title: Add Python C extension module for syntaqlite
items.body:
> Clone https://github.com/LalitMaganti/syntaqlite to /tmp - then figure out how to build a Python c extension module that exposes the core features of that tool directly to Python. Also build a WASM wheel bundle for this, look at similar examples in this repo to see how that can work. Target the most recent Pyodide for that (figure out the correct emscripten version to use etc). Build this all with red/green TDD and 'uv run pytest' to run tests.

## Summary

This PR adds a complete Python C extension module that exposes syntaqlite's core functionality (parsing, formatting, validation, and tokenization) to Python. The implementation includes native builds and WASM wheel support for Pyodide.

## Key Changes

- **C Extension Module** (`_syntaqlite.c`): Wraps four core APIs from syntaqlite:
  - `parse()` - Parse SQL into AST with error recovery
  - `format_sql()` - Format SQL with configurable line width, indentation, keyword casing, and semicolons
  - `validate()` - Validate SQL against optional schemas with "did you mean?" suggestions
  - `tokenize()` - Tokenize SQL into individual tokens (including whitespace and comments)
  - Custom `FormatError` exception for format failures

- **Build Configuration** (`setup.py`, `pyproject.toml`): 
  - Links against pre-built static libraries from syntaqlite (`libsyntaqlite.a`, `libsyntaqlite_engine.a`, `libsyntaqlite_sqlite.a`)
  - Supports both native builds and emscripten cross-compilation for WASM wheels
  - Handles platform-specific linking (macOS Security framework, Linux pthread/dl/m)

- **WASM Build Support** (`build_wasm.sh`):
  - Cross-compiles Rust libraries to `wasm32-unknown-emscripten`
  - Uses pyodide-build to package as Pyodide-compatible wheel
  - Includes workarounds for emscripten path length limits and wasm-opt version mismatches

- **Comprehensive Test Suite** (`test_syntaqlite.py`): 35 tests covering:
  - Parse: simple/multiple statements, syntax errors, complex queries
  - Format: keyword casing, semicolons, line width, indentation, error handling
  - Validate: schema validation, "did you mean?" suggestions, rendered diagnostics
  - Tokenize: token text/offsets/types, whitespace, comments

- **Documentation** (`README.md`): Complete API reference, build instructions for native and WASM wheels, architecture overview, and version compatibility matrix

## Implementation Details

- All memory is properly managed (parser/formatter/validator instances created and destroyed per call)
- Python C API used throughout for compatibility with both CPython and Pyodide
- Error handling includes proper exception propagation and resource cleanup
- WASM build copies static libraries to short paths to avoid "argument list too long" errors
- Emscripten-specific flags (`-sWASM_OPT=0`) to work around LLVM version mismatches

items.title: Defer C extension imports to fix SIGILL on module load
items.body:
The __init__.py was eagerly importing amrex.space3d and _core at module level, which meant ANY import from the openimpala package (including pure-Python modules like cli.py) triggered loading the compiled C extensions. On machines where the container dependencies were built with -march=native for a different CPU, this caused Illegal Instruction crashes during pytest collection.

Now __init__.py uses a module-level __getattr__ to lazy-load _core and its symbols only when actually accessed. facade.py similarly defers _core imports to function bodies via _get_core(). Pure-Python imports (Session, exceptions, CLI parser) work without the C backend.

items.title: Add matrix_perf_compare: C vs C++ (Eigen) compile-time optimization benchmark project
items.body:
Introduces a complete, buildable benchmark project comparing runtime-only C matrix operations against Eigen-backed C++ with compile-time optimizations (fixed-size, expression templates, fused operations).

## Project Structure

- **`include/c_matrix/matrix.h`** — Row-major `Matrix` struct; full C API
- **`src/c/`** — `matrix.c`, `ops.c`, `benchmarks_c.c`: 9 ops, `clock_gettime` harness, CSV output
- **`src/cpp/benchmarks_cpp.cpp`** — Three benchmark groups: dynamic Eigen, fixed-size Eigen (`N=3/4/8/16`), expression template fusion
- **`CMakeLists.txt`** — C11/C++17, `-O2`, FetchContent Eigen 3.4.0, CTest; targets: `c_matrix_bench`, `cpp_matrix_bench`, `c_matrix_tests`, `cpp_matrix_tests`
- **`tests/`** — C correctness suite (20 assertions) + C++ cross-validation against C results
- **`scripts/`** — `run_all.py` orchestrator; `summarize_results.py` generates Markdown comparison tables from CSVs
- **`docs/`** — `methodology.md` (fairness rules, design), `compiler_explorer.md` (5 Godbolt-ready snippets)

## Operations Covered

`transpose`, `add`, `sub`, `scale`, `matvec`, `mul`, `transpose_mul`, `add3` (`A+B+C`), `mul_add` (`A*B+C`) — at sizes 32×32, 128×128, 512×512 (dynamic) and 3×3–16×16 (fixed).

## Key Design Points

Both implementations use the same LCG seed for deterministic, reproducible fills:
```c
// C and C++ share identical fill logic
s = s * 1664525u + 1013904223u;
val = (double)((int)(s >> 8) % 10000) * 0.0001;
```

Expression template fusion example (C++ avoids temporary in `A+B+C`; C always materializes intermediates):
```cpp
// Single traversal — Eigen defers evaluation until assignment
Out = A + B + C;
```
vs.
```c
// Two passes, explicit temp required
matrix_add(&a, &b, &tmp);
matrix_add(&tmp, &c, &out);
```

Fixed-size benchmarks are intentionally separated from dynamic ones in results to avoid conflating compile-time specialization wins with apples-to-apples runtime comparisons.

# Agentic Task: Create a Benchmark Project Comparing Compile-Time-Optimized C++ Matrix Operations vs Runtime-Only C Matrix Operations

## Goal

Create a complete, buildable, benchmarkable example project that compares two approaches to matrix operations:

1. **C++ implementation**

   * uses a high-performance C++ matrix library
   * demonstrates compile-time optimization techniques
   * includes operations from **transpose** to **matrix multiplication**
   * should emphasize zero-cost abstractions, expression fusion where available, fixed-size optimizations where appropriate, and compile-time dimension handling

2. **C implementation**

   * uses C-only matrix handling
   * runtime-only behavior
   * same operations and as-close-as-possible identical semantics
   * no compile-time specialization beyond ordinary compiler optimization
   * intended as a fair baseline

The project must be suitable for:

* local benchmarking
* Compiler Explorer excerpts
* article/demo material
* educational comparison of generated code and runtime results

---

## High-Level Outcome

The repository must produce:

* one **C++ benchmark executable**
* one **C benchmark executable**
* one **shared benchmark report generator or summary script**
* one **README** explaining the purpose, methodology, fairness rules, and findings
* a set of **small, medium, and large test cases**
* optional **assembly inspection targets** or instructions for Godbolt/Compiler Explorer extraction

---

## Main Comparison Theme

The project must compare:

* transpose
* matrix addition
* matrix subtraction
* scalar multiply
* matrix-vector multiply
* matrix-matrix multiply
* chained expressions such as:

```text
C = transpose(A)
D = A + B
E = A * B
F = transpose(A) * B
G = A + B + C
H = A * B + C
```

The point is not only raw multiplication, but also showing where C++ compile-time-rich code can:

* eliminate temporaries
* fuse expressions
* exploit fixed-size knowledge
* inline aggressively
* reduce indirection

while the C version remains:

* explicit
* runtime-oriented
* manually structured
* fair, but not magically templated

---

## Technology Requirements

### C++ Side

Use one of the following libraries:

Preferred:

* **Eigen**

Acceptable alternatives:

* Blaze
* Armadillo

Selection criteria:

* header-only or easy integration
* strong compile-time optimization story
* well-known in performance/numerics world
* suitable for fixed-size and dynamic-size matrix examples

The C++ implementation must include:

* **fixed-size matrices** where dimensions are known at compile time
* **dynamic-size matrices** for fairer comparison with C
* at least one benchmark showing benefit of expression templates / fused operations

### C Side

Use:

* plain C implementation
* no C++ wrappers
* no template-like code generation tools
* optional BLAS backend only if clearly separated as an additional variant, not the baseline

Baseline C implementation must:

* use heap-allocated or stack-allocated raw buffers
* implement matrix operations manually
* keep dimensions runtime-driven
* avoid obviously pathological code

---

## Fairness Rules

The benchmark must be fair and explicitly document fairness assumptions.

Required fairness rules:

1. Same matrix dimensions for matching tests
2. Same element type per benchmark group

   * use `double` by default
3. Same number of warmup runs
4. Same iteration counts
5. Avoid dead-code elimination by consuming results
6. Use equivalent optimization flags where possible
7. Separate:

   * fixed-size compile-time-optimized C++ tests
   * dynamic-size C++ tests
   * runtime-only C tests

The README must explicitly explain:

* why fixed-size C++ is expected to win in some cases
* why dynamic-size C++ vs C is a more apples-to-apples comparison
* why the project includes both

---

Adapt if needed, but keep it clean and documented.

* optional MSVC support is welcome

items.title: Optimize cost function: vectorized eta + C extension rasterizer
items.body:
## Summary
- **Vectorize eta filtering loop**: Replace 2K-iteration Python for-loop with batched PyTorch operations (torch.bmm + torch.atan2). Eliminates per-iteration scalar math and tensor creation.
- **C extension for rasterization**: New `_rasterize.c` CPython extension implementing Sutherland-Hodgman clipping + Bresenham scanline fill + pixel overlap counting in C with zero-copy numpy array access. Used by both `image_data.py` (triangle overlap) and `cost_functions.py` (pixel radius overlap).
- **Overall speedup**: `evaluate()` 8,824 us → 3,589 us (**2.46x faster**). All 328 tests pass.

items.title: Fix C extension module import on Linux
items.body:
## Description

This PR fixes issue #471 where third-party C extension modules (.so) fail to import on Linux while working on Windows.

## Problem

On Linux, `dlopen("TestPk")` does NOT automatically add `.so` suffix, unlike Windows where `LoadLibraryA("TestPk")` automatically searches for `TestPk.dll`. Additionally, CMake typically generates `libTestPk.so` (with `lib` prefix) instead of `TestPk.so`.

## Solution

Modified `load_module_from_dll_desktop_only()` in `src/interpreter/dll.c` to try multiple library name patterns on Linux:

1. First try: `{path}.so` (e.g., `TestPk.so`)
2. Second try: `lib{path}.so` (e.g., `libTestPk.so`) - CMake default naming
3. Fallback: original path (for explicit paths like `./mylib.so`)

## Changes

- Added `stdlib.h` and `string.h` includes for `malloc`, `strcpy`, `strcat`
- Added logic to construct and try alternative library paths on non-Windows platforms
- Proper memory management with `malloc`/`free`

## Testing

This fix allows the following import patterns to work on Linux:

```python
import TestPk  # Will try TestPk.so, then libTestPk.so
```

Where `TestPk` is a C extension module compiled with:
- `TestPk.so` - direct compilation
- `libTestPk.so` - CMake default output

## Related Issue

Fixes #471

items.title: fix: force aws-c-* deps to shared to avoid duplicate aws-c-common copes in JNI builds
items.body:
aws-sdk-cpp 1.11.692 requires aws-c-common at runtime regardless of the s3-crt option. Conan defaults to building aws-c-* as static libraries with hidden visibility, so each libaws-cpp-sdk-*.so ends up embedding its own private copy of aws_default_allocator. Aws::InitAPI (linked into libaws-cpp-sdk-core.so) only initializes core's copy, leaving the copy inside libaws-cpp-sdk-s3.so uninitialized. The first S3Client construction then aborts inside aws_mem_acquire:

Fatal error condition occurred in
.../aws-c-common/0.12.5/.../allocator.c:202: allocator != NULL

Stack trace (abridged):

libaws-cpp-sdk-s3.so   DefaultEndpointProvider<S3ClientConfiguration>::C1
libaws-cpp-sdk-s3.so   Aws::S3::S3Client::C2
libmilvus-storage.so   ClientBuilder::BuildClient
libmilvus-storage.so   S3FileSystem::Make
libmilvus-storage.so   S3FileSystemProducer::Make    (InitS3() already ran)
libmilvus-storage.so   FilesystemCache::get
libmilvus-storage.so   loon_transaction_begin

Verified with `nm libaws-cpp-sdk-s3.so | grep aws_default_allocator`:
- before: `t aws_default_allocator` (private static copy per .so)
- after:  `U aws_default_allocator` (resolved dynamically from libaws-c-common.so.1)

The monolithic milvus binary does not hit this because all aws-cpp-sdk sub-modules are linked into a single ELF, so there is only one aws_default_allocator. Any downstream that splits aws-cpp-sdk into separate .so files (e.g. the JNI fat jar consumed by spark-connector) trips on it.

Fix: force the entire aws-c-* family to be built as shared libraries via a wildcard option, so all aws-cpp-sdk-*.so dynamically link a single libaws-c-common.so.1. Requires Conan >= 1.41 for wildcard keys in default_options. The existing package_fat_jar.sh already copies SONAME-versioned `*.so.*` files (since #477), so the new libaws-c-*.so.1 artifacts are picked up automatically with no script change.

items.title: Replace pybind11 with nanobind and optimize wrapper performance
items.body:
## Summary
- Migrate C++ binding layer from pybind11 to nanobind (lighter, modern alternative by the same author)
- Optimize Python wrapper with lazy log evaluation, zero-copy ndarray, and fast C++ accessors
- **49% faster** on ex04 benchmark (11x11 grid, 12100 vehicles): 3.66s → 1.86s
- Add CI wheel build workflow, bump version to 1.14.0b2

## Details

### nanobind migration
- `bindings.cpp`: `namespace py` → `nb`, `NB_MODULE` macro, `rv_policy`, `ndarray`-based numpy helpers, granular STL headers (`nanobind/stl/vector.h`, `map.h`, etc.)
- `CMakeLists.txt`: `nanobind_add_module` + `find_package(nanobind)`
- `pyproject.toml`: build dependency `pybind11` → `nanobind`
- CI workflows: updated install commands
- Wrapper: `float()`/`bool()` casts at call sites for nanobind's stricter type checking (no runtime cost — called once during scenario setup)

### Performance optimizations
- **Lazy log evaluation**: Vehicle logs stored as raw numpy arrays from C++ batch API. Converted to Python objects (strings, Link refs) only on first access. Eliminates `_convert_raw_log` bottleneck (was 36% of total runtime).
- **Zero-copy ndarray**: `make_numpy_move<T>()` transfers `std::vector` ownership via `nb::capsule` instead of `memcpy`.
- **Fast C++ accessors**: `Link.vehicle_count` (O(1) `deque.size()`), `Link.avg_speed` (computed in C++), `World.link_vehicle_count(id)`.
- **Wrapper**: `get_vehicle_by_index` for O(1) vehicle lookup, link id-based reference instead of name-based dict lookup.

### Benchmark (ex04, 11x11 grid, 7200s, 12100 vehicles, 10 runs)
| Version | Median | Improvement |
|---------|--------|-------------|
| pybind11 (before) | 3.66s | baseline |
| nanobind + optimizations | 1.86s | **49% faster** |
| pybind11 + same optimizations | 1.82s | 50% faster |

Performance gain is primarily from wrapper optimizations (lazy eval, etc.), not from nanobind itself. nanobind vs pybind11 performance difference is <3% (within noise).

## Test plan
- [x] All 172 C++ mode tests pass (`pytest tests/test_cpp_mode.py --reruns 5`)
- [x] Benchmark verified on ex04 scenario
- [x] Profile confirms `_convert_raw_log` bottleneck eliminated
- [ ] CI wheel builds on all platforms (ubuntu/macos/windows)

Part of https://github.com/toruseo/UXsim/issues/297
